author: @alexsanders7481
comment:
It is fascinating how connectionists spent years dismissing Symbolic AI, only to end up trying to reinvent it. What they now call an "AI Agent" is just a basic symbolic module that has existed in cognitive architectures for decades.
On my desk lies a 100-page A4 manual, typed in 10pt font, detailing how to construct these exact modules: 50 pages for the Core Module rules and methodologies, and another 50 pages for External Modules. This knowledge was accumulated over decades by multidisciplinary experts: cognitivists, linguists, ontologists, and software engineers.
Yet modern connectionists, who lack basic understanding of proper module architecture, claim they can teach people how to build symbolic modules for their AI in a "10-hour course." While symbolists spent decades learning through rigorous practice, connectionists pretend it can be mastered in 10 hours by some magic. This is a blatant deception.
I have a question for the author of this video. It is a well-known fact that these so-called "AI agents" (which are essentially poorly constructed symbolic modules) have already caused massive financial and reputational damage to companies. Do you really want people to lose even more money because of this lack of understanding?
Here is what the actual data shows:
1. Air Canada: Their autonomous support AI agent independently invented a fake refund policy, forcing a passenger into a lawsuit. The court rejected the airline's defense and ordered them to pay C$812.02 in damages and court fees, setting a legal precedent where companies are fully liable for their AI agents' hallucinations.
2. Chevrolet of Watsonville: Their sales AI agent, built on pure LLM logic, was manipulated into making a legally binding offer to sell a $76,000 Chevy Tahoe for just $1. The dealership suffered an immediate brand crisis with 20+ million viral views, forcing them to shut down the tool to prevent massive direct financial losses.
3. PocketOS: In April 2026, an autonomous coding AI agent (powered by Claude Opus 4.6 in Cursor) encountered a minor credential mismatch. Operating entirely on its own initiative without a strict, deterministic Core Module to block destructive behavior, the agent misused an API token and deleted the startup's entire live production database and all volume-level backups in just 9 seconds, leaving the company in total chaos and forcing them to roll back to a 3-month-old archive.
